1.synthesis:
Moses, flanked by the elders of Israel, issues a solemn command for the day Israel crosses the Jordan: they are to erect large plastered stones on Mount Ebal and inscribe on them "all the words of this law" — a monument both durable and publicly visible (vv. 2-3). Beside this law-monument, they are to build an altar of unhewn fieldstones and offer burnt offerings and peace offerings, eating and rejoicing before the LORD (vv. 6-7). The conjunction of written law and sacrifice is not incidental: it announces that no one can stand before the inscribed demands of God without the mediating altar close by.

Keil and Delitzsch note that the chapter opens the final section of Deuteronomy — the formal closing of the Sinai covenant in Moab — and that the Ebal ceremony was to occur immediately upon entering the land, "in token of their taking possession of it under the several provisos and conditions contained in this law." Calvin observes the deeper symbolic purpose: as God required the law on doorposts of individual houses, so the law is to be inscribed at the very entrance of the land, making Canaan itself a sanctuary of heavenly doctrine. Matthew Henry draws out the juxtaposition: "In the Old Testament the words of the law are written, with the curse annexed, which would fill us with horror and amazement if we had not in the New Testament an altar erected close by it, which gives us everlasting consolation." The fact that the altar is on Ebal — the mount of cursing — deepens the message: the altar stands precisely where the curse is named, offering atonement in the same place judgment is declared.
1.historical_context: Mount Ebal and Mount Gerizim face each other across a narrow valley at Shechem in the central highlands, forming a natural amphitheater. Shechem was already a place of covenant significance: Abraham built an altar there (Gen 12:6-7) and Jacob bought land there (Gen 33:18-19). The choice of this site for covenant ratification links the Mosaic covenant to the Abrahamic promises. The plastered-stone inscription technique is attested in Egyptian and Near Eastern practice; the Deir ʿAllā inscription from Transjordan is a later analog. Matthew Henry notes the "monument" was mean in materials — common plaster, not polished marble — yet vast in inscription, emphasizing accessibility of the law to all.
1.literary_structure: Verses 1-8 form a unified command about two things to be done simultaneously at the Jordan crossing: the inscribed stones (vv. 2-4, 8) and the altar with offerings (vv. 5-7). The passage is bracketed by "you shall do" and "you shall write" — law and liturgy inseparably joined. Moses and the elders speak together (v. 1), then Moses and the Levitical priests speak together (v. 9-10), showing the command comes from both civil and religious authority. "When you have crossed over the Jordan" (v. 2) — the ceremony belongs to the threshold moment, defining the terms under which Israel enters the land.
1.theological_application: Matthew Henry notes three things signified by the feast of rejoicing at the altar: consent to the covenant (eating together ratifies an agreement), comfort in the covenant (there is joy when law meets altar), and confession of dependence on God rather than the land itself. Calvin emphasizes that the unhewn altar-stones were not to be dressed by iron tools — a principle that excluded human craftsmanship from altering what God appoints: "Christ, our Altar, is a stone cut out of the mountain without hands, refused by the builders, as having no form or comeliness, but accepted of God the Father." The law's plain inscription was intended to prevent the excuse of ignorance — every person who entered Canaan was entering under written terms.
1.christology: Matthew Henry's observation that the altar on Ebal "was set up on Mount Ebal, the mount on which those tribes stood that said Amen to the curses, to intimate that through Christ we are redeemed from the curse of the law" is the passage's central christological claim. Paul quotes Deuteronomy 21:23 in Galatians 3:13: "Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us." The altar on the mount of cursing prefigures the cross: the place of judgment becomes the place of atonement. The altar of unhewn stones — shaped by no human hand — anticipates the stone "cut out without human hands" of Daniel 2:34-35 and the rejected cornerstone of Psalm 118:22, both applied to Christ by the New Testament.

9.synthesis:
With the altar ceremony established, Moses and the Levitical priests now call upon all Israel to attend — "Take care and listen, O Israel: this day you have become the people of the LORD your God" (v. 9) — and then divide the twelve tribes across the two mountains for the liturgical ceremony of blessings and curses. Six tribes (Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, Joseph, Benjamin) stand on Gerizim for blessing; six (Reuben, Gad, Asher, Zebulun, Dan, Naphtali) stand on Ebal for the curse (vv. 12-13). The Levites then recite twelve curses, each targeting a category of sin — idolatry in secret, dishonoring parents, removing boundary stones, misdirecting the blind, oppressing the alien/orphan/widow, sexual immorality, murder for hire, and finally a comprehensive curse on anyone who does not confirm "all the words of this law" (v. 26). To each, all the people cry "Amen."

Ellicott's commentary notes that the ceremony was not a division into blessers and cursers — all the people answered Amen to both sets — but a liturgical antiphony: the tribes received the blessing and the curse on behalf of the whole nation. Calvin emphasizes that the public recitation placed every Israelite under a self-pronounced sentence: they ratified their own condemnation for transgression with their own voices. Matthew Henry underscores that the twelve curses address primarily secret sins — those done in the dark, outside human oversight — because these are the sins most tempting when no human court can reach them.
9.historical_context: Ellicott's commentary explains the geography and mechanics: six tribes on the southern hill (Gerizim), six on the northern (Ebal), with the priests and Levites and the ark in the valley between. The Levites turned alternately toward each mountain, pronouncing first the blessing, then the curse, and "all the people shall say Amen." The twelve curses correspond roughly to sins against the three tables of the law: the first (idolatry) against God, several against neighbor (boundary removal, misdirection, oppression, murder, sexual sin), and the final catch-all against the entire law. Joshua fulfilled this ceremony as commanded in Joshua 8:30-35, shortly after the conquest of Ai.
9.literary_structure: The twelve curses form a structured literary list, each beginning with "Cursed is the one who..." and ending with the congregational "Amen." The list is not random: it begins with the most fundamental sin (idolatry in secret, v. 15) and ends with the most comprehensive statement (failure to keep the whole law, v. 26). Between these brackets stand sins that share the quality of hiddenness or vulnerability of the victim — the blind, the alien, the widow, the orphan. The structure reflects the law's concern for those who cannot protect themselves and the God who sees what courts cannot reach.
9.theological_application: Calvin notes that the curses reach precisely where human law cannot: hidden sins, private acts that injure neighbors without witnesses. The public Amen to each curse made every Israelite acknowledge they had placed themselves under divine judgment for offenses no human court could adjudicate. Matthew Henry draws the pastoral application: "The curse is a very solemn thing, and not to be made light of; it is God's wrath, which burns to the lowest hell." Adam Clarke finds in the final curse (v. 26) — "Cursed is everyone who does not confirm the words of this law by doing them" — the root of Paul's argument in Galatians 3:10: the law places every person who fails to keep it entirely under its curse, which is every person without exception.
9.christology: Paul quotes verse 26 in Galatians 3:10 as the foundation of his argument about the law's curse: "For all who rely on works of the law are under a curse; for it is written, 'Cursed be everyone who does not abide by all things written in the Book of the Law, and do them.'" The congregational "Amen" at Mount Ebal was Israel's corporate acknowledgment that every member stood under this curse — and therefore needed the redemption announced in Galatians 3:13: "Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us." Matthew Henry remarks that the altar on Ebal was placed precisely at the mount of curses, so that sacrifice for sin could be offered where the curse was pronounced — a pattern fulfilled when Christ, hanging on the tree, exhausted the curse and opened the way to the Gerizim blessings for all who believe.
